sentences.base_language: I get up and wash my face.
I brush my teeth for two minutes.
sentences.grammarNote: Duration in Japanese is expressed with a time word + 間 (kan): 二分間 (for two minutes), 一時間 (for one hour). Unlike Chinese (which repeats the verb for duration), Japanese simply places the duration phrase before the verb.

sentences.base_language: My hair is long and black.
sentences.target_language: 私の髪は長くて黒いです。
sentences.romanized: Watashi no kami wa nagakute kuroi desu.
sentences.grammarNote: The て-form of い-adjectives connects two qualities: 長くて黒い (long and black). The pattern is: adj (drop い, add くて) + next adjective. This is the Japanese equivalent of the Chinese 又…又… (both…and…) pattern for listing adjectives.

着る (kiru) = 'to put on / wear (upper body clothing)'. Japanese has different verbs for wearing different items: 着る (shirts, coats), 履く (haku) for pants/shoes, 被る (kaburu) for hats. Using the wrong verb sounds unnatural.

sentences.target_language: ようやく準備ができました。
sentences.grammarNote: 準備ができる (junbi ga dekiru) = 'to be ready / preparations are complete'. できる here means 'to be completed/done'. ようやく (yōyaku) = 'finally / at last', with a sense of relief after effort. とうとう (tōtō) is a similar word but often used for inevitable outcomes.

出る (deru) = 'to exit / leave / go out'. 家を出る (ie wo deru) = 'to leave the house'. The を here marks the place being exited — a special use of を. Compare: 家に入る (enter the house) with 家を出る (leave the house).
